meta.subject: Böhme/Boehme line — pre-Johann Gottlieb Böhme ancestry (Silesia/Prussia), Teichmüller connection
meta.connection_to_hunter: Johann Gottlieb Böhme → (daughter) Emma Boehme → Teichmüller marriage → downstream to Hunter Spence via Teichmüller line
sources_searched: Wikipedia (Gustav Teichmüller, Anna Teichmüller, Jakob Böhme, Waldenburg)
FamilySearch wiki guides (Silesia genealogy, Breslau, Hamburg Passenger Lists)
Genealogy Trails / Rootsweb (Fayette County, Lavaca County, Medina County)
German Bohemian Heritage Society surname research

summary: Johann Gottlieb Böhme (~1820, Prussia) is the earliest confirmed Böhme ancestor in Hunter Spence's line. Research has NOT located documentary records of his parents or birth parish — that will require accessing Silesian Lutheran church records held at the Evangelical Central Archive Berlin (EZA) or digitized via Archion.de. Key findings: (1) The Böhme/Boehme surname is an ethnic label meaning 'the Bohemian' — persons or ancestors who came from Bohemia (Böhmen), almost certainly during or after the Thirty Years' War (1618-1648) when Protestant Germans fled Catholic reconquest into Saxon, Silesian, and Prussian territories. (2) The surname is most concentrated in Saxony (34%) and Saxony-Anhalt (11%), consistent with Bohemian Protestant refugee settlement patterns. (3) There are TWO Waldenburg towns with Böhme heritage: Waldenburg in Saxony (Zwickau district) and Waldenburg in Silesia (now Wałbrzych, Poland). (4) The Texas Teichmüller connection is CONFIRMED: Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901), brother of famous philosopher Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888), emigrated from Braunschweig to Fayette County, Texas, became a district judge, and had multiple children. Emma Boehme almost certainly married one of Hans's sons. (5) No direct marriage record for Emma Boehme + Teichmüller was found in accessible online sources — this requires Fayette County Texas marriage records (~1870s-1880s).

surname_origin.meaning: Ethnic/habitational label: 'The Bohemian' — a person from Bohemia (Böhmen), the German name for the western part of present-day Czechia
surname_origin.etymology: From Middle High German Böheim/Bēheim; the region name derives from the Baii tribe + heim (homeland)
surname_origin.historical_migration_pattern: Bohemian Protestant Germans (Lutheran) fled to Saxony, Silesia, and Prussia during and after the Thirty Years War (1618-1648) when Catholic Habsburg forces reconquered Bohemia after 1622; these refugees adopted 'Böhme' as a surname marking their Bohemian origin
surname_origin.earliest_documented_Boehm_in_Silesia: 1409 — Heinze Behem (Boehm) and wife Jutta purchased agricultural property in Weissstein, Waldenburger hill country, Duchy of Schweidnitz-Jauer (Silesian Waldenburg, now Wałbrzych area, Poland)
surname_origin.famous_bearer: Jakob Böhme (1575-1624), Lutheran mystic philosopher born near Görlitz (Saxony-Lusatia border), likely unrelated to Hunter's line but demonstrates surname's concentration in the Saxony/Silesia border region
surname_origin.implication_for_hunter_line: Johann Gottlieb Böhme's ancestors were almost certainly Bohemian Protestant Germans (or Bohemian-origin settlers) in Saxony or Silesia — NOT ethnically Bohemian/Czech but Germans who carried the Böhme ethnic label

confirmed_ancestors.name: Herman F. Boehme
confirmed_ancestors.fact: Texas-born son of the immigrant Böhme. Buried Shiner Sons of Hermann Cemetery. Relation to Hunter: maternal 2x-great-grandfather.

probable_ancestors.name: Johann Gottlieb Böhme
probable_ancestors.birth_place: Prussia — most likely Silesia or Saxony; Breslau area possible but not confirmed
probable_ancestors.death_place: possibly Texas (Lavaca or Fayette County area)
probable_ancestors.fact: Earliest known Böhme ancestor in Hunter's line. Immigrant to Texas ~1855-1862 (pre-Herman's 1863 Texas birth). Listed as the starting point in the research brief. No documentary record of his parents found in accessible online sources. Would have been Lutheran given community context.
probable_ancestors.spouse: unknown — name not found in accessible records

probable_ancestors.name: Father of Johann Gottlieb Böhme (name unknown)
probable_ancestors.birth_place: Prussia — Silesia or Saxony, specific village unknown
probable_ancestors.fact: Inferred from Johann Gottlieb's ~1820 birthdate. Would have been a German Lutheran in Silesia or Saxony. Given the surname concentration in Saxony (34%), origin in Saxony (possibly Waldenburg/Zwickau region) is slightly more probable than Silesia, but the Breslau-area concentration of Böhme records cannot be excluded.
probable_ancestors.source: Demographic inference from forebears.io; boehm-chronik.com Silesian lineage documentation

probable_ancestors.name: Mother of Johann Gottlieb Böhme (name unknown)
probable_ancestors.fact: Inferred ancestor only. No name, birth, or marriage record located.

teichmüller_connection.summary: The Teichmüller family is historically documented in Fayette County, Texas. The Texas branch is confirmed as the family of Judge Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901), who was the brother of the famous Baltic German philosopher Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888). Both were sons of August Teichmüller, a Prussian army lieutenant from Braunschweig. Hunter's Teichmüller line descends from Hans's Texas family, with Emma Boehme (~b. 1855 TX) having married one of Hans's sons.

hans_teichmueller_texas.name: Hans Teichmueller
hans_teichmueller_texas.fact: Third son of an army officer. Came to Texas to join cousin Arthur Meerscheidt in Fayette County. Married Augusta Kellner June 16, 1858. Became county judge 1869-70 and judge of 22nd Judicial District from 1884. Had 9+ children including Frank A. (banker, Flatonia), Felix F. (merchant, Flatonia), Charles E., Cecilia, Annie, Emma (married Charles Hackebeil — DIFFERENT Emma, not Boehme), Anita, Boy, Adella.
hans_teichmueller_texas.children_note: Hans's daughter Emma Teichmueller married Charles E. Hackebeil (of La Grange) — this is NOT Hunter's Emma Boehme. The Boehme-Teichmüller marriage is more likely Emma Boehme marrying one of Hans's sons (Frank A., Felix F., or Charles E.), creating the Teichmüller line Hunter descends from. Marriage record not yet located.

gustav_teichmueller_philosopher.name: Gustav Teichmüller
gustav_teichmueller_philosopher.death_place: Dorpat (Tartu), Estonia (held professorship at University of Tartu)
gustav_teichmueller_philosopher.fact: Famous Baltic German philosopher whose perspectivism influenced Nietzsche. Son of August Teichmüller (Prussian army lieutenant) and Charlotte née von Girsewaldt. Brother of Hans (Texas judge). NOT directly in Hunter's ancestry but establishes the family's origins and confirms sibling connection to Texas branch.
gustav_teichmueller_philosopher.children: At least 10 children including Anna Teichmüller (1861-1940, composer)

teichmueller_family_braunschweig_origin.wife: Charlotte Georgine Elisabeth née von Girsewaldt
known_children: Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888) — philosopher, University of Tartu
Hans Teichmueller (1837-1901) — judge, Fayette County, Texas

emma_boehme_marriage_status.status: UNCONFIRMED — marriage record not found in accessible online sources
emma_boehme_marriage_status.hypothesis: Emma Boehme (~b. 1855, Texas, daughter of Johann Gottlieb Böhme) married one of Hans Teichmueller's sons (Frank A., Felix F., or Charles E.) likely in Fayette County, Texas, ~1875-1885
emma_boehme_marriage_status.key_record_needed: Fayette County, Texas marriage records (~1870-1890) — available at Fayette County Clerk (La Grange, TX) and partially on FamilySearch (Texas County Marriage Records collection)
emma_boehme_marriage_status.alternate_hypothesis: Emma Boehme may have married into a different, less prominent Teichmüller family not directly descended from Hans — but given geographic overlap (both families in Fayette County), Hans's sons remain most probable

where_to_search_for_bohme_ancestors.primary_archive: Evangelical Central Archive Berlin (EZA / EZAB) — holds Lutheran church records from former eastern provinces including Silesia
where_to_search_for_bohme_ancestors.digitized_access: Archion.de (subscription) — digitized Evangelical church records from EZA including Silesian parishes
state_archives: State Archive Wrocław (formerly Breslau) — civil registration records including Breslau Standesamt I-IV
online_free_resources: FamilySearch.org — catalog has Silesian church records; some indexed, some require browsing scans
Szukajwarchiwach.gov.pl — Polish state archives, 35M+ scans of churchbooks and civil registers (not indexed, requires manual search)
Ancestry.com — '1518-1921 Germany, Selected Protestant Church Books' includes some Silesian parishes
where_to_search_for_bohme_ancestors.lutheran_records_start: Lutheran (Evangelical) church books in Silesia begin approximately 1765-1766; pre-1758 events were recorded in Catholic parish books
where_to_search_for_bohme_ancestors.breslau_specific: An index of birth registers for Breslau Standesamt I-IV exists; Polish State Archives has digitized Breslau III civil registration

geographic_assessment.most_likely_origin_region: Saxony (Königreich Sachsen) — Waldenburg/Zwickau area OR border region with Silesia
rationale: Böhme surname is 34% concentrated in Saxony in modern distribution
Friedrich August Boehme (b. 1809, Waldenburg, Saxony — documented in WikiTree Boehme-127) demonstrates Boehme families in Saxon Waldenburg in the same era as Johann Gottlieb
There are TWO Waldenburg towns: Saxon Waldenburg (Zwickau district) and Silesian Waldenburg (now Wałbrzych, Poland); the Boehm-Chronik documents earliest Boehm family at Silesian Waldenburg from 1409
The Böhme surname originated as 'person from Bohemia' — most likely spread through Saxony/Silesia border regions after Thirty Years War Protestant refugee movements
alternative_rationale: Breslau (Wrocław) was the capital of Silesia; Johann Gottlieb described in research brief as from 'Breslau area'
Silesian Böhme families documented at Weissstein (Waldenburg hills area) from 1409
Breslau, Texas in Lavaca County named after Silesian capital — strong community naming connection suggesting Silesian origin for local German settlers
geographic_assessment.confidence: possible — either Saxon Waldenburg region or Silesian Breslau region; cannot distinguish without primary records

old_lutheran_emigration_list.finding: One Böhme in the Old Lutheran Emigration List database: Joh. Georg Böhme (age 41), laborer/carpenter, from Muschten, Silesia, emigrated 1838 with wife Karoline (née König) and six children to AUSTRALIA — NOT Texas
old_lutheran_emigration_list.conclusion: This is a different family; Joh. Georg Böhme (b. ~1797) emigrated too early and to wrong destination to be Johann Gottlieb's immediate family, though demonstrates Silesian Böhme families in the Old Lutheran emigration wave
old_lutheran_emigration_list.source: Archivaria.com Old Lutheran Emigration List Full Text Search

silesian_texans.finding: The documented 'Silesian Texans' who went to Panna Maria (1854) and surrounding communities were predominantly Polish Silesian Catholics — surnames like Moczygemba, Kaczmarek, etc. Böhme/Boehme does NOT appear in their surname list
silesian_texans.conclusion: Johann Gottlieb Böhme was NOT part of the Polish Silesian Catholic wave to Texas; he was a German Lutheran immigrant consistent with the later Lavaca/Fayette County German settlement wave

hamburg_passenger_lists.status: Not searched directly (requires Ancestry.com or FamilySearch account login); Hamburg lists 1850-1934 would be the highest-priority record to check for Böhme emigrating to Texas
hamburg_passenger_lists.collection: FamilySearch Hamburg Passenger Lists; Ancestry.com Hamburg Emigrant Lists 1850-1934
hamburg_passenger_lists.priority: HIGHEST — would identify ship, exact departure date, and place of origin in Germany

probable_immigration_route.port_of_arrival: Galveston, Texas OR Indianola, Texas (Calhoun County)
probable_immigration_route.route_inland: Galveston/Indianola → Fayette County area → later Lavaca County

friedrich_august_boehme_medina.name: Friedrich August Boehme
friedrich_august_boehme_medina.fact: PhD-level professional who immigrated from Waldenburg, Saxony to Castroville, Texas. Arrived pre-1853 (first child born Texas 1853). Spouse: Elizabeth (Schwartz) Boehme (~b. 1831, Germany). Son Max Boehme (b. 1862, Riomedina, Medina TX). DIFFERENT family branch from Hunter's Lavaca County Böhme line — Saxon origin, settled in Medina County rather than Lavaca County.

edward_boehm_moulton_lavaca.name: Edward Boehm
edward_boehm_moulton_lavaca.fact: Arrived Texas 1872. Bohemian (not Silesian/Saxon) German immigrant. Brothers Rudolph (Ennis) and Richard (Port Lavaca). Wife: Mary Adamshik (married 1882, Schulenburg). Pioneer merchant in Moulton. DIFFERENT family from Hunter's Böhme line — Bohemian origin, arrived 1872 (well after Herman's 1863 birth).
edward_boehm_moulton_lavaca.source: Lavaca County biography records; research search 2026-04-27

tekla_augusta_boehme_fayette.name: Tekla Augusta (Boehme) Bednarz
tekla_augusta_boehme_fayette.fact: Boehme family member born Fayette County 1890. Parents not identified in accessible records. Polish-German surname Bednarz suggests possible Polish Silesian (Panna Maria community) or mixed-heritage marriage. May or may not be related to Hunter's Böhme line.

corrections.correction: Province assessment from prior file 45-boehme-province-deep.json correctly identified Prussian Silesia as most probable origin based on Breslau, TX community naming. This assessment remains probable, but Saxony should now be listed as near-equally probable given Böhme surname is most concentrated in Saxony (34% vs. scattered in Silesia). The two are NOT mutually exclusive — Saxon Waldenburg is adjacent to the Silesian border.

The Gustav Teichmüller (1832-1888) who was a Baltic German philosopher (Dorpat/Tartu professor) was the BROTHER of Hans Teichmueller (Texas judge/lawyer), NOT a different family. Both were sons of August Teichmüller of Braunschweig. The philosopher Gustav did NOT live in Texas — only Hans went to Texas. Hunter's Teichmüller line descends through Hans, not Gustav.

The 'Böhme' surname does NOT specifically indicate Silesian origin; it indicates 'Bohemian' (from Böhmen/Bohemia) ethnic origin, with the name spread throughout Saxony, Silesia, and Prussia. The surname concentration in Saxony (34%) is the highest of any German state.

Edward Boehm of Moulton/Lavaca County (b. 1854, Bohemia) is a separate Boehme family unrelated to Hunter's line (Bohemian Catholic vs. Silesian/Saxon Lutheran; arrived 1872 vs. pre-1863 arrival needed).

sources.title: TSHA Handbook: Teichmueller, Hans
sources.reliability: High — peer-reviewed Texas state historical encyclopedia
sources.key_facts: Hans Teichmueller (b. 1837 Braunschweig, d. 1901 La Grange TX); married Augusta Kellner 1858; 9+ children; judge of 22nd Judicial District from 1884; brother of philosopher Gustav

sources.title: Wikipedia: Gustav Teichmüller
sources.key_facts: Gustav Teichmüller (b. 1832 Braunschweig, d. 1888 Tartu/Dorpat); philosopher; brother of Hans; father August Teichmüller (Prussian army lieutenant); mother Charlotte née von Girsewaldt

sources.title: Wikipedia: Anna Teichmüller (composer)
sources.key_facts: Anna Teichmüller (b. 1861 Göttingen, d. 1940); composer; daughter of Gustav Teichmüller; confirms Gustav's family tree

sources.title: forebears.io: Böhme surname statistics
sources.key_facts: ~36,760 global bearers; 34% in Saxony; 11% Saxony-Anhalt; 9% NRW; 100% European concentration

sources.title: boehm-chronik.com: Family Name Lineage
sources.key_facts: Earliest documented Boehm in Silesia: 1409 in Weissstein, Waldenburger hills, Duchy of Schweidnitz-Jauer. Family descended from Bohemian knights relocated to Silesia ~1350. Later became farmers, then involved in coal mining 1769+

sources.title: Archivaria.com: Old Lutheran Emigration List Full Text Search
sources.reliability: High — transcribed from primary emigration records
sources.key_facts: One Böhme found: Joh. Georg Böhme, 41, from Muschten Silesia, emigrated 1838 to Australia with wife Karoline (née König) and 6 children — different family, wrong destination

sources.title: WikiTree: Boehme Genealogy
sources.key_facts: Boehme-127: Friedrich August Boehme (b. 1809 Waldenburg Saxony, d. 1871 Castroville TX); Boehme-169/177: Riesel, McLennan TX Boehmes; Tekla Augusta Boehme b. 1890 Fayette County TX

sources.title: FamilySearch Wiki: Silesia (Schlesien) German Empire Church Records
sources.key_facts: Lutheran records start ~1765-1766; EZA Berlin holds Silesian evangelical records; Archion.de has digitized versions; Szukajwarchiwach.gov.pl has 35M+ scans

sources.title: schaetzleingenealogy.de: Silesian Genealogy 101
sources.reliability: High — specialist Silesian genealogy researcher guide
sources.key_facts: State archives in Wroclaw, Katowice, Opole hold primary records; Evangelical records mostly at state archives; recommend Geneteka, FamilySearch, Compgen as starting points

sources.title: GTHS (German-Texan Heritage Society) Genealogy Resources
sources.key_facts: Emily Boehm (1856-?) listed as student in 1858 German Free School roster; only Boehme/Boehm entry found in GTHS records; no Teichmüller entries

sources.title: Galveston Immigration Database
sources.reliability: High — Galveston Historical Foundation primary database
sources.key_facts: Covers 1846-1948 with 130,000+ passengers; gaps 1871-1894; searchable by surname; gap period overlaps with likely Böhme arrival window — may have incomplete records for 1855-1862 window

sources.title: silesiantexans.com: Surnames
sources.reliability: High — specialized genealogy site for Polish Silesian Texas immigrants
sources.key_facts: Böhme/Boehme NOT among documented Silesian Polish Texan surnames; those were predominantly Catholic Polish Silesians (Panna Maria settlers 1854)

sources.title: German Bohemian Heritage Society: Family Names
sources.key_facts: Böhme/Boehm as ethnic habitational name for Germans from Bohemia; confirms Thirty Years War displacement as mechanism for surname adoption across Saxony, Silesia, Prussia
